text: Translevator robotic abdominal perineal resection for recurrent rectal cancer after low anterior resection. A six-year-old woman underwent trimodality therapy for locally advanced rectal cancer. She was found to have a low pelvic anastomotic recurrence approximately one year after diagnosis, just after the time of ileostomy reversal. This is the area of the anastomotic recurrence with ulceration. Biopsy revealed moderately invasive adenocarcinoma. Cross-sectional imaging with computed tomography and PET scan was obtained and identified an area of high activity on CT PET scan.
The surgeon mobilizes the proximal neorectum laterally and medially during the translevator robotic abdominal perineal resection, carefully identifying and preserving the left and right ureters.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and cautery dissection to remove adhesions from the left uterine vessels, posterior uterus, and vagina. The adhesions appear relatively soft, allowing for minimally invasive dissection despite previous pelvic surgery.

Following the completion of anterior dissection, the surgeon performs proximal mesenteric division to enhance retraction of the neorectum. The left ureter, equipped with a temporary ureteral catheter, is palpated and visually identified.

The surgeon initiates posterior dissection, mobilizing the neorectum or colon off the previously dissected total mesorectal excision plane. The adhesions are minimal and are managed using a minimally invasive technique.

The surgeon initiates translevator dissection on the right posterior lateral portion of the surgical field, targeting an area with previous scar tissue at the level of the prior anastomosis and local recurrence.

The posterior midline is exposed, revealing the distal sacrum and coccyx. The surgeon continues the posterior cylindrical translevator dissection, encountering tissue with soft edema due to previous radiation, which increases the difficulty of dissection. The dissection proceeds posteriorly to the left and lateral.
The surgeon continues the posterior translevator dissection, followed by a biomanual examination to confirm the level of distal dissection both transrectally and transvaginally.

The surgeon completes the dissection in a transperineal fashion, extracting the specimen transperineally. The view shows the completed dissection from both the abdominal and perineal sides, highlighting the wide translevator cylindrical excision.
The uterus is released from retraction, and the pelvis is occluded. The final pathology specimen is displayed, showing internal structures on the left and posterior dissection on the right.

Recurrent rectal cancer is typically managed with a radical open approach due to the dense pelvic adhesions, local tumor extension, and complexity of the operation. However, in select patients with low line and asthmatic recurrence confined to the rectum, a minimally invasive approach may be feasible.
